title: Google I/O 2025 – VTCIStB6y8s
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] UNA KRAVETS: Hi, everybody. Thank you for coming. Welcome to What's New in Web UI. I'm Una, a Chrome Developer
Relations engineer focused on all things
visual on the web platform, so that means CSS,
HTML, and JavaScript, that helps you build beautiful,
engaging web interfaces. We've come a long way
in the past five years. Incredibly powerful
features that we didn't even know we could have
in the browser have not only landed,
but have also gained cross-browser compatibility
at a faster pace than they ever have before. The HasParent selector,
container queries, color functions,
view transitions, nesting, trigonometric
functions, scroll driven animations,
it's truly amazing what you can now do with
native HTML and CSS. How lucky are that
we get to experience this shift, this
golden age of web UI, this really cool era where
we get to be web developers? That being said, there are still
some really common UI patterns, components that we use and
build every day, that are still really, really
hard to get right, and as a developer
community, we've relied heavily on JavaScript
frameworks or complex CSS tricks, and sometimes
mountains of custom code to create these UI patterns
that feel like they should be a lot simpler. And that's precisely
where the Chrome team is focusing its efforts,
making those surprisingly complex, but really
common, UI patterns that are fundamental in the platform
straightforward to implement. Today, we're going
to zero in on three of those notoriously tricky
patterns, the endlessly frustrating customizable
select, the deceptively complex carousel, and the
interactive hover card. These components may
look simple to a user, but us developers
know the strife of trying to get the
accessibility right in the carousel buttons, or
needing to completely rebuild a select with divs just to
change the font or the colors. And don't even get
me started on trying to create these hover
cards that you see all over the place in web UIs. The good news is
that the Chrome team
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – VTCIStB6y8s
text:
a select with divs just to
change the font or the colors. And don't even get
me started on trying to create these hover
cards that you see all over the place in web UIs. The good news is
that the Chrome team has been working with other
browser vendors, the web community, and standards bodies
like the CSS working group and What Working Group, along
with community groups like Open UI, to standardize solutions
to deliver powerful declarative APIs that tackle these
challenges head on. So there is good news
there, and in this talk, I'm going to explore how
upcoming browser features make building these patterns a lot
simpler, more performance, and more accessible. And these features are landing. Everything that I'm going
to talk about here today, with the exception of
one feature at the end, has shipped in at least
one stable browser engine. And I also want to note that,
while it may not look complete yet, those features that
have landed in Chrome are also a part of a
process called Standards, and we hope that they'll
also ship across browsers. So we'll be using baseline
terminology and symbols to help you understand feature
availability, so look out for these throughout the talk. And also, if you didn't
catch What's New in Web yesterday, you can
definitely catch that online to learn more
about baseline and features that have landed. So first, let's start
with the Select element. The Select is essential
for countless forms, but the internal
structure has historically been obfuscated by the browser,
effectively shielding it from consistent and
comprehensive CSS styling across different
browsers and platforms. But before we even
get into the Select, we need to talk about its
building blocks, which are popover and anchor positioning. Not only are these features
the underlying tech behind the customizable
Selects, but because of their primitive nature,
they can do so much more. So first, let's talk
about the dropdown list itself, that floating
box of options. You need it above
everything else in the UI,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – VTCIStB6y8s
text:
behind the customizable
Selects, but because of their primitive nature,
they can do so much more. So first, let's talk
about the dropdown list itself, that floating
box of options. You need it above
everything else in the UI, easy to dismiss, and
manage focus when it opens, and customizable
Select leverages the super useful popover
API to do just that. I talked about popover
on this stage last year, and I'm excited to announce
that popover has now reached baseline newly available
status this year, so it's in all browsers. Really exciting to see it
go from one to all of them in a year. So as an example, here, we
have the YouTube notifications pop over just for a visual. And a way to create a
popover is fairly simple. You just need two pieces. The first is a trigger
element, like this button here, and the second is the
actual popover itself. All you need to do is
give the popover an ID and set the popover
attribute on that popover. Then you connect the popover
to its invoking button using popover target like this. Behind the scenes,
the popover API isn't just showing an element. It's managing its
entire lifecycle. Popover automatically
supports top layer rendering, so you don't
have to fight with z-index and stacking context
issues anymore. It provides a built
in light dismiss functionality on
auto popovers, so that when a user clicks outside
of the popover area, it closes. It automatically
manages focus where the browser handles tab
navigations for the popover when it's opened and
correctly returns tab focus to the invoking
element when it's closed, ensuring predictable
keyboard navigation regardless of where the popover
is in the dom structure. And while you still need to
choose the correct element, whether that's a dialogue,
or apply the correct ARIA role to define the
element's semantic purpose, like a menu or a tooltip, the
underlying interaction model is handled natively. These declarative features
have been so well received and work so well that it
makes it really easy to reach
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – VTCIStB6y8s
text:
role to define the
element's semantic purpose, like a menu or a tooltip, the
underlying interaction model is handled natively. These declarative features
have been so well received and work so well that it
makes it really easy to reach for a popover, but that
might not always be the best choice for your component. For example, when you
have an interaction that blocks the rest of the page
and requires some sort of user action, the layered UI element
should really be a modal dialog. But the dialog element was
built before popover was, so it lacked some of these neat
features like light dismiss and a nice declarative
API that can be triggered without JavaScript, until now. Now, we have the closedby
attribute landing a few months ago in Chrome 134. Users can now use
closedby equals any to enable light dismiss
directly on modal dialogs. With closedby equals any,
clicking outside of the dialog or pressing the Escape
key closes the dialog. This seemingly small interaction
is such a game changer for simplifying light
dismiss behavior, and it's awesome to see this
land for the dialog element. Another area where
repetitive JavaScript is, it's connecting the
button click to an action, like opening that dialog. Command invokers provide a
declarative HTML solution similar to popover target. Here, a button invokes
a command dialog. This is done by using the
command and commandfor pattern, you can use the very
straightforward command showmodal, which is akin to the
JavaScript showmodal function. Then you target the
dialog by referencing its ID in the command
for attribute, like so. You can also use this to
close the dialog using the close command. This command commandfor
pattern gives us a cleaner way to link elements
and actions in HTML, removing boilerplate JavaScript
for many standard behaviors like that opening and
closing functionality. And this can be used on any
element, dialog and beyond. The browser automatically
manages ARIA details for you, eliminating the need
for extra JavaScript.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – VTCIStB6y8s
text:
removing boilerplate JavaScript
for many standard behaviors like that opening and
closing functionality. And this can be used on any
element, dialog and beyond. The browser automatically
manages ARIA details for you, eliminating the need
for extra JavaScript. For non-supported browsers,
there is a polyfill, too, and all you have to do is import
this file, and it just works. It's pretty cool. I've used it, and I've checked
it on non-supporting browsers. So let's put this into context
and refactor an existing dialog demo that Adam Argyle designed
to be more declarative. I really like this
demo because it has that cool x that shows
you're closing the dialog, but here, what
you see we've done is we've just removed
the JavaScript, and it's not closing. It's not working when
you click outside. We have to add
closedby equals any. So once we've added
that, now when we open the dialog and
click outside of it, the light dismiss is enabled
declaratively in HTML without JavaScript. [APPLAUSE] Next, what we can do is get rid
of the rest of this boilerplate JavaScript. So here, I'm removing these
inline onclick commands, and I'm replacing them with
command and commandfor. Commandfor is linked to
the ID of the dialog, and the command
here is showmodal. Now, when I click,
it opens that dialog without the inline JavaScript. And I can do the same thing
for that Close button. So I'm showing you here,
as I'm opening the dialog, and I hit the Close
button, nothing is happening because
we've removed the script. But all we have to
do is add command equals close here, and
then commandfor, that demo, which is the ID of that dialog. It's linked right there. Now, all of the
functionality here, opening it, closing it,
light dismissing it, is built in in HTML without
any JavaScript needed. It's pretty cool. [APPLAUSE] So now, we can update this
table with the new capabilities that we now have for dialog. With command invokers
and closedby equals any, you can focus on the semantic
needs of your UI elements
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – VTCIStB6y8s
text:
It's pretty cool. [APPLAUSE] So now, we can update this
table with the new capabilities that we now have for dialog. With command invokers
and closedby equals any, you can focus on the semantic
needs of your UI elements and leave the interactions
to the browser. Remember that a
dialog is an element, and popover is an
attribute that still needs more semantic context. But the Open UI
community group continues to work on shipping
semantic UI components like a possible future menu
element with the ability to easily create
nested menus like so. So we've deviated a bit,
but it's all good stuff. Let's get back to
the Select menu, which we've
established decidedly uses a popover for the
listbox of options. But how does that
popover listbox appear relative to the
button that opened it? Well, positioning it
correctly is important, and that is what anchor
positioning is for. This CSS API declaratively
tethers one element to another, handling edge collision
automatically. Last year on this stage, I
introduced anchor positioning and talked about its
powerful capabilities, and now browser
support is expanding. In fact, anchor
positioning is one of the features that is
a part of Interop 2025, an annual cross-browser
initiative to fix bugs and land highly requested developer
features as quickly as possible. This means we expect
anchor positioning to land in all browsers
by the end of this year. Fingers crossed. In addition to
anchor positioning, this year's Interop
list includes backdrop filter, the details
element, addscope, scroll end event, text decoration, view
transitions for single page apps, writing modes, and more. And we've seen a lot of progress
on these features already, including anchor positioning. There's a lot of
cool things that you can do with CSS anchor
positioning, the most basic of which is, well,
anchoring things together. Who could have guessed? And you may have noticed,
in the previous demo, that the notifications
and popover are anchored to this little bell icon. You can anchor any
element together
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – VTCIStB6y8s
text:
basic of which is, well,
anchoring things together. Who could have guessed? And you may have noticed,
in the previous demo, that the notifications
and popover are anchored to this little bell icon. You can anchor any
element together using anchor name and the
position anchor properties, like this. But as of Chrome
133, you no longer need to do this for
popover elements and their invoking buttons. The browser creates an
implicit anchor relationship. This makes the code for building
anchored popovers much cleaner, so I'm thrilled to see this
change land in the spec and roll out across browsers
as this feature lands. Now, all we need to
do to create an anchor is use one line of CSS, like
you see here, to position it. So here, we're using
position-area bottom span-left, which is positioning it at
the bottom of that anchor and spanning towards the left. This language is pretty
straightforward for positioning and spanning grid lines,
and to make it even easier, I built this tool for
you as a reference, which you can find at
Chrome.dev/anchortool. You can also switch
to logical properties and instead of values-- instead of the
directional values to accommodate
for writing modes, and you can also see
the containing block. This is the inset
modifier containing block where that anchor
is being positioned. So that is one of the
interesting key details of anchor positioning. It has this inset
modified containing block. This helps you
visualize where it is. Initial positioning is
one part of the equation for our notifications
bar, but what if the user resizes the
screen, or we decide to shift where that bell icon is? We could reposition where it is
in an action bar, for example. We don't want our popover to
get cut off in the viewport. I've had to implement logic
like this before in the past, and it was not fun. Took a couple of days. Luckily, the browser can now
handle that for you, too. You can specify
fallback positions like this one, which I've
created and called left,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – VTCIStB6y8s
text:
I've had to implement logic
like this before in the past, and it was not fun. Took a couple of days. Luckily, the browser can now
handle that for you, too. You can specify
fallback positions like this one, which I've
created and called left, which spans from left to right
instead of right to left, or use browser defaults like
flip block and flip inline. Then you can call
it as a fallback when you can't position
from that initial right to left position. Now, you can feel confident
that, no matter where this notifications trigger
bell lives in your UI, the popover will
stay on the screen and be visually optimized. This is especially
important for layouts that make heavy use of layered UI. Building something
like this yourself requires resize observers,
which are slow to calculate, and a lot of complex
logic that you simply don't have to worry about
now, and you can let the browser handle it for you. So I think this is
an awesome feature. [APPLAUSE] For more about the
anchor positioning API, definitely check
out this link where you'll find more in-depth
feature coverage and demos. So coming back to our
customizable Select menu, styling the native
Select has always been the core challenge
here, and this is really what inspired the work behind
popover and anchor positioning. But we got some great
primitives along the way. It still feels like
something like this should be so easy to build, but
achieving this really custom look takes you down
a complex road. You essentially have
to hide the real Select and build a complete replacement
using divs and lists, and then you need
a lot of JavaScript to make this fake component
behave like a real Select, handling clicks,
keyboard navigation, syncing values, and manually
adding the necessary ARIA attributes for accessibility. It's brittle and a huge
amount of work to get right, and even then it doesn't
work with autocomplete, which we heard yesterday is
essential to business model for many businesses out there. But native styling for Select
elements is finally here,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – VTCIStB6y8s
text:
It's brittle and a huge
amount of work to get right, and even then it doesn't
work with autocomplete, which we heard yesterday is
essential to business model for many businesses out there. But native styling for Select
elements is finally here, and I'm excited to
announce that it has reached a stable
release in Chromium 134. So 2017 Una would be very
proud of this right now. [APPLAUSE] It seems so simple, but
it's a really big deal. Native styling for Select
comes with its own slew of interesting features like a
new appearance property, picker pseudo, picker icon, and a new
element called selected content. First, the way to enter into
the customizable select mode is by using the
appearance property and setting it to base select. You do this on
the Select itself, and on a new pseudo
element called the picker. Picker is a functional pseudo
element that will eventually accept other picker
types like color, but it essentially
targets the popover, which contains the list of options. So in Select, it's
that dropdown list. Using appearance
based Select unlocks a number of new
features and behaviors, including exposing new internal
parts and states of the Select, showing options in the
top layer as a popover, and positioning them
with anchor positioning. It will also simplify
the user agent style, as you can see here, so
you can bring your own. You now have access to full
built-in styling for the Select dropdowns button, options,
checked option, and even a new pseudo element
for a checkmark which indicates the selected option. All of this together
gives you a ton of unique control over
customizing Select, and in a much easier, more
straightforward way than before. Take this example, which is
similar to Google AI Studio's picker menu. Here, I have a button where
I'm inserting selected content, and each option has a slot for
an icon, title, and subtitle. One neat thing
that I'm doing here is making the selected
content, which shows the option that I picked
only show the name of the Gemini
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – VTCIStB6y8s
text:
Here, I have a button where
I'm inserting selected content, and each option has a slot for
an icon, title, and subtitle. One neat thing
that I'm doing here is making the selected
content, which shows the option that I picked
only show the name of the Gemini version, so I'm hiding the rest
of the content in that option, like the subtitle and
icon, which would otherwise get reflected in the
selected content. And I'm doing this by
simply setting it to display none in that selected content. I'm also bringing in a
custom background image using a Base64 encoded
image for that checkmark to indicate the
checked item, and I'm repositioning it from the
user-agent default styles. One of the coolest things about
reusing the existing Select element for this feature is the
progressive enhancement story. In a complex Select
like this one, this currency
picker, when you're using the customizable Select in
browsers that have this feature, you can see what it
looks like on the left. But in non-supported browsers,
you still get meaningful content that the user can select,
and you get autocomplete, and you get all of
those native defaults that the browser provides
for you for free. That's cool. [APPLAUSE] But of course, the most
significant benefit is that you can achieve this
customized look while preserving the native Select elements,
built-in accessibility, keyboard navigation, and
form integration. You're not throwing
away the browser's work. You're just changing
how it looks. And to help you use it
correctly in Chrome 137, you will be able to see
warnings in DevTools if you violate Accessibility
Guidelines when building customizable Selects,
like having interactive elements inside of options. So we want to make sure
that you use this right. Temu is one of
our partners who's already been exploring
the customizable Select and has provided some
really great feedback, and we've been working with
them on enabling next generation functionality for Selects
like this type filtering functionality.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – VTCIStB6y8s
text:
already been exploring
the customizable Select and has provided some
really great feedback, and we've been working with
them on enabling next generation functionality for Selects
like this type filtering functionality. The next big UI challenge
is carousels and other off screen user interfaces. Love them or hate them,
carousels are all over the web. Product showcases,
image galleries, onboarding flows,
they are incredibly useful for fitting related
content into tight spaces, and building them
is, well, it's hard. It's not easy. We wrestle with scripting for
state management, scroll jank, interactivity,
cross-browser compatibility, and getting the
accessibility right. Along the lines of today's theme
of a more powerful web made easier, now the
browser can, again, handle much of that
complexity for us. But really, when
you think about it, carousels are just
fancy scroll areas with some extra UI affordances
like buttons and markers to manage navigation
and pagination. So let's make a carousel. The way to get started is by
creating some content that flows off screen,
but now, we have this dreaded
horizontal scroll bar. Who's fought with this before? I've fought with these. Yeah, a lot of hands-- a lot of hands are
up in the room. One neat little trick that
just reached baseline widely available is
scrollbar-width none, so you can feel confident
using this feature. I'm happy that this landed. To make a UI maintain that
horizontal scrollability without a visible
scroll bar, we no longer have to do some weird
negative margin hacks. Now, you can just set
scroll bar width to none, and poof, like that,
the scroll bar is gone, and the content is
still scrollable. Just one of those
little big things. With a few lines
of CSS here, I've set overflow, removed
that scroll bar visually, and given our carousel an
anchor name, because that will come in handy in a minute. And using scroll-snap-type
and scroll-snap-align, which are both widely
available since January 2020, I've enabled item
by item snapping
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – VTCIStB6y8s
text:
and given our carousel an
anchor name, because that will come in handy in a minute. And using scroll-snap-type
and scroll-snap-align, which are both widely
available since January 2020, I've enabled item
by item snapping to the center of the carousel. Now that we have
our scroller set up, let's start with our first
scroll navigation affordance, and that is Previous
and Next buttons. The new scroll button pseudo
element makes this easy for you, but it doesn't just provide an
entry point to style a button. It also tells the
browser to generate connected stateful button
elements for navigating this scroll container, including
a disabled state when you can't scroll anymore in
a given direction, like you can see
here at the start. That's the real magic
with CSS scroll buttons. The browser automatically gives
these buttons the correct ARIA role, puts them
in the tab order, and disables them at
the start or the end, so you don't need to write any
scripting to do all of this. You can instantiate the buttons
like other pseudo elements, with a selector, scroll button
right as a button on the right, and scroll button
left for the left. You can also use top, bottom,
and logical properties like button-- inline start, and you can
style both at the same time with an asterisk
in the argument. You need to add content
to give the pseudos a meaningful, accessible
label, which can be done using this slash syntax. So look how simple that is. Just make your container
scrollable, add these rules, and boom, you have
navigation buttons. And now you can lay out these
buttons however you want, including an apparent grid or
using CSS anchor positioning. Remember how we just set an
anchor name on our carousel? Well, this is where it's
going to come in handy. We'll need to use
that anchor name to anchor the Previous and
Next buttons in the carousel, like so. We're using position area,
again, to position them. Just like the Page Down
button on your keyboard, scroll buttons shift the
content in the carousel
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – VTCIStB6y8s
text:
to anchor the Previous and
Next buttons in the carousel, like so. We're using position area,
again, to position them. Just like the Page Down
button on your keyboard, scroll buttons shift the
content in the carousel by 85% of the scroll
port by default, but you can have
more precise control by using scroll-snap-type
and scroll-snap-stop. For example, the
code here will now only shift the carousel
one item at a time when you click the buttons. So Previous and Next
buttons are a great start, but what about
direct navigation? Think those dot
indicators or thumbnails. This is where
scroll-marker comes in. I like this naming because it
aligns with marker elements for list items, too,
scroll-marker and scroll-marker-group. Scroll-marker lets you
associate navigation markers directly with the items inside
of your scroll container. To add a scroll-marker,
similar to scroll buttons, you can use a pseudo
element that is initiated with the content property. Be sure to give these
accessible labels as well, like I'm doing here,
and what I prefer doing is using a data attribute. So for each individual
item, I could have a data name
on that list item, and then I could pull it into
the content as its label, using the attribute
function like so. So now, I'm dynamically reading
the data name on each item and giving that label to all
of these navigation dots. This is a great feature. I love using attr,
and it's a great use case when you're using it
for labeling like this. But the attribute function also
got a big upgrade in Chrome 133, so it's even more powerful now. Before, attr was a way to
pull string content from data attributes to display as
content in pseudo elements, so it was pretty
limited to strings. But now, you can use data
attributes in a much more meaningful way and cast
the values to colors, numbers, percentages, custom
identifiers, and so on. It's a really cool feature, too. Yeah. So take this example
of a rating UI. We're setting the
rating directly as a data attribute in
this div, and if you're

title: Google I/O 2025 – VTCIStB6y8s
text:
numbers, percentages, custom
identifiers, and so on. It's a really cool feature, too. Yeah. So take this example
of a rating UI. We're setting the
rating directly as a data attribute in
this div, and if you're using a framework,
which you probably are, this is likely how you're
doing it, with props where you're setting the data rating. We already have
this information. So now, using data-rating, you
can reference this numeric value and use it in your styles. Here, we're using
attr data-rating. We're giving it a
type number, so we're casting it to the number. And we're doing
some calculations to create that fill percentage,
which is filling in a mask. These stars are a
mask, and now, we're using that number of the rating
to create the percentage that we want to fill the mask. We're doing this all in one
line of code in that CSS custom property. So we can now use that
value inside of a gradient and give that
gradient a hard stop to actually visualize
that fill percent. This is a trick I use all the
time with linear gradients and that hard stop value. Super useful. We can even reuse that data
attribute in the content to display the value. So I think that this
extended powerful typed capability for the attr
function is awesome, and it's really cool. I think this really showcases
how powerful CSS is getting. And speaking of
powerful CSS, let's get back to our scroll markers. After we've initialized the
scroll marker with content, we have scroll markers that
are placed in the scroll marker group, which is
the element where they're going to be created. This is some browser
magic that does this, because scroll
marker is attached to each item in your
carousel scroller. So every list item, or
every div or every iframe, however you've
created the carousel, will get a scroll marker,
but the scroll-marker-group lives inside of the carousel
parent, so it's kind of funky. If you start
building with these, you can explore in
DevTools, and you'll see the difference
of where they live versus how they're attached.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – VTCIStB6y8s
text:
will get a scroll marker,
but the scroll-marker-group lives inside of the carousel
parent, so it's kind of funky. If you start
building with these, you can explore in
DevTools, and you'll see the difference
of where they live versus how they're attached. But it kind of
needs to be that way so that you can do
the accessible labels and connect it to
all of your items. The browser treats the
scroll-marker-group like a tab list, and
the markers like links. It even handles the
keyboard navigation between markers using arrow
keys just like a focus group. Finally, there is a new pseudo
class primitive to style the active marker
called target-current. You can use target-current to
style the active scroll marker. In this case, we're giving
it this blue background. With just these
three CSS features, we've set up all
of the state logic, keyboard navigation,
identified the active marker, hooked up all of its content,
and enabled full stylability of carousel marker indicators. Again, this is incredibly
powerful with very minimal CSS. Another neat scroll marker
demo is this product card. It uses image URLs in the scroll
markers as a background image to provide a preview of the
item that you're about to use, and you can do this because
you can use a background image for each
scroll marker that's connected to each list item. So that's a pretty
neat feature to see, and again, it's just a
scrollable list of items that we're creating using
these carousel primitives. A basic carousel
could definitely look a little bit nicer with
a touch of animation design, and since we've established
that a carousel is a fancy scroller now, let's
use some fancy new scroll APIs. From scroll state queries
to scroll driven animations, there have been a lot
of new scroll features that have landed in the
past few months, which make it possible to make
carousels just beautifully interactive. This brings me to a new
type of MediaQuery called scroll-state queries,
and I'm so thrilled to see this feature
landing because it solves

that have landed in the
past few months, which make it possible to make
carousels just beautifully interactive. This brings me to a new
type of MediaQuery called scroll-state queries,
